coredata.dc:title: On-off-on nanosensors of carbon quantum dots derived from coal tar pitch for the detection of Cu2+, Fe3+, and L-ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Chaoshuai
Zhu, Yaming
Zhao, Xuefei
coredata.dc:description: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by a chemical oxidation method using medium-low temperature coal tar pitch as the raw material. Such quantum dots were developed as a highly sensitive fluorescent “on-off-on” switch sensor for the selective and simultaneous sensing of Cu2+ and Fe3+. The as-prepared N-CQDs, which emit blue light, were characterized by TEM images, FT-IR spectra, Raman spectroscopy, XPS analysis, fluorescence spectra, and UV–vis absorption spectra. The results showed that the N-CQDs exhibit outstanding optical properties and high optical stability within the pH range of 4–10, with a quantum yield of approximately 7%. Additionally, the material performed as an “on-off” sensor which can be dramatically extinguished by Cu2+ and Fe3+. A linear relationship between Cu2+ and Fe3+ ion concentration and fluorescence intensity was observed in the range from 0 to 50 μM. The limits of detection of the fluorescent sensor toward Cu2+ and Fe3+ were 0.16 μM and 0.173 μM, respectively. The Fe3+-quenched N-CQDs were restored after adding L-ascorbic acid, due to the redox reaction between Fe3+ and L-ascorbic acid, which resulted in the detachment of Fe3+ from the surface of the N-CQDs. The linear range for the detection of L-ascorbic acid was 0–28 μM. Therefore, the amount of L-ascorbic acid can be measured by using the sensing system consisting of Fe3+ and N-CQDs. In consequence, N-CQDs are considered an important material for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in water samples or L-ascorbic acid in drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Detection
Quenching
Recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs by Cu2+ and Fe3+
Response of N-CQDs to Cu2+and Fe3+
N-CQDs for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+
Detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in real samples
N-CQDs-Fe3+ for the detection of AA
Detection of AA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University of Science and Technology Liaoning

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of carbon quantum dots prepared by different precursors at 365 nm excitation. A) HS. B) TS. C) QS.
A) TEM image, B) Particle size distribution, C) FT-IR spectrum, and D) Raman spectrum of N-CQDs.
A) XPS survey, B) C1s, C) N1s, and D) O1s of N-CQDs.

A) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra (λex = 365 nm) of the N-CQDs solution; the inset shows the corresponding photographs of N-CQDs solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light. B) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQD solution under different excitation wavelengths. C) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 365 nm) of N-CQDs at different concentrations. D) Influence of pH on the FL intensity of N-CQDs at an excitation of 365 nm.
A) Relative fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the presence of different metal ions (100 μM). B) Photographs of N-CQDs in the presence of different metal ions under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation.
A) Selectivity of N-CQDs for Cu2+ coexisting with 100 μM of other ions. B) Selectivity of N-CQDs for Fe3+ coexisting with 100 μM of other ions.

A and C) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs solution at different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by 365 nm excitation wavelength. B and D) Relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Fe3+ and Cu2+, insets show a linear relationship within the range of 0–50 μM.

A) Relative fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs containing L-ascorbic acid and vitamin C Lozenge. B) Relative fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and Cu2+ and N-CQDs and Fe3+ mixture after the addition of L-ascorbic acid. C) Recovery of fluorescence intensity after the addition of L-ascorbic acid in the hybrid system (80 μM Fe3+ and 0.03 g/L N-CQDs). D) The linear relationship between FL intensity and the concentration of L-ascorbic acid (0–28 μM) in the hybrid system (80 μM Fe3+ and 0.03 g/L N-CQDs).
A) Proposed structure of N-CQDs. B) Absorption spectra of N-CQDs, N-CQDs + Fe3+, and N-CQDs + Cu2+, the inset shows the enlarged 310–383 nm range.
Schematics of the development of N-CQDs from TS by the nitric acid reflux method, and Cu2+/Fe3+ and L-ascorbic acid detection using the N-CQDs.
Determination of Fe3+ in samples with the proposed method.
Determination of Cu2+ in samples with the proposed method.
Determination of L-ascorbic acid in drugs with the proposed method.

head.ce:title: On-off-on nanosensors of carbon quantum dots derived from coal tar pitch for the detection of Cu2+, Fe3+, and L-ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology Liaoning

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Metallurgy Liaoning Province’, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Metallurgy Liaoning Province’
University of Science and Technology Liaoning

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Metallurgy Liaoning Province’, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Chemical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology Liaoning

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by a chemical oxidation method using medium-low temperature coal tar pitch as the raw material. Such quantum dots were developed as a highly sensitive fluorescent “on-off-on” switch sensor for the selective and simultaneous sensing of Cu2+ and Fe3+. The as-prepared N-CQDs, which emit blue light, were characterized by TEM images, FT-IR spectra, Raman spectroscopy, XPS analysis, fluorescence spectra, and UV–vis absorption spectra. The results showed that the N-CQDs exhibit outstanding optical properties and high optical stability within the pH range of 4–10, with a quantum yield of approximately 7%. Additionally, the material performed as an “on-off” sensor which can be dramatically extinguished by Cu2+ and Fe3+. A linear relationship between Cu2+ and Fe3+ ion concentration and fluorescence intensity was observed in the range from 0 to 50 μM. The limits of detection of the fluorescent sensor toward Cu2+ and Fe3+ were 0.16 μM and 0.173 μM, respectively. The Fe3+-quenched N-CQDs were restored after adding L-ascorbic acid, due to the redox reaction between Fe3+ and L-ascorbic acid, which resulted in the detachment of Fe3+ from the surface of the N-CQDs. The linear range for the detection of L-ascorbic acid was 0–28 μM. Therefore, the amount of L-ascorbic acid can be measured by using the sensing system consisting of Fe3+ and N-CQDs. In consequence, N-CQDs are considered an important material for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in water samples or L-ascorbic acid in drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which consist of a carbonaceous core and surface passivation groups [1], are a new type of “quasi-zero dimensional” fluorescence small carbon nanoparticles with a particle size of less than 10 nm [2]. In the past few years, carbon quantum dots have gained increasing attention due to their versatile properties, including facile synthesis, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, low price, and especially, excellent optical properties [3–5]. Oxygen-containing and other heteroatom-doped functional groups connected to their surface make CQDs highly soluble in water and suitable for ion detection and biological imaging applications. Therefore, CQDs have been applied in many fields, such as medical imaging technology [6–8], environmental monitoring [9–12], chemical analysis [13,14], catalyst preparation [15–17], optoelectronic devices [18,19] and energy storage [1,20,21].

Copper and ferrum are essential trace elements for animals, plants, and humans. However, the excess of Fe3+ in living organisms can cause methemoglobinemia, mental decline, physical weakness, and other diseases. Excessive Cu2+ can damage plant cell structure and membrane systems, affect the growth and development of aquatic animals, and induce human liver cancer. Therefore, the monitoring of Cu2+ and Fe3+ is vital for environmental and health purposes. Such ions can be monitored using different strategies, such as carbon quantum dot fluorescence analysis [22–24], atomic absorption spectroscopy [25], and inductively coupled plasma analysis, [26] among others. More specifically, detection using spectroscopic analysis results in higher precision and accuracy. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) form complexes with metal ions, which change the fluorescence intensity for ion detection [27–29]. For example, Zhang et al. prepared CQDs by a hydrothermal method with black sesame as the carbon precursor and the dual-function fluorescent sensor can detect Fe3+ and L-ascorbic acid [23]. Reza Tabaraki et al. developed carbon quantum dots by a microwave-assisted method handling the mixture of citric acid, urea, and thiourea which can simultaneously detect Hg2+ and I− as a dual off-on sensor [30].

L-ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, is an important vitamin necessary to maintain key physiological functions. In addition, L-ascorbic acid is involved in a variety of physiological functions, including reducing blood cholesterol content, enhancing immunity, and increasing capillary elasticity. Lack of AA can lead to scurvy and excessive amount of AA can induce gastritis and cause skin eruptions. Therefore, facile and high accuracy sensors to detect AA are vital for the early precaution of diseases caused by L-ascorbic acid. Moreover, CQDs possess broad application prospects for detecting AA among numerous detection methods [23,31–33]. Wang M. et al. synthesized carbon dots with orange peels as a precursor by a hydrothermal method. The dots were modified with EDTA and combined with Cr(VI), which acted as an “off-on” sensor for the detection of L-ascorbic acid with a LOD of 0.1 μM [34]. Hu G. et al. successfully obtained carbon quantum dots developed from flax straw by a simple hydrothermal method. The fluorescence of the CQDs was quenched by Co2+ and Cr6+, and the mechanism was interpreted as a static quenching and inner filter effect. The subsequent CQDs + Cr6+ composite system was able to detect AA due to the oxidization of Cr6+, resulting in the quenched composite system being destroyed and fluorescence being restored [35].

In this work, medium-low temperature coal tar pitch (MLP) was used as the starting material for the development of CQDs. The pitch was extracted by using n-heptane, toluene, and quinoline as extracting agents to obtain different soluble group components. The three soluble compositions were consequently used as carbon precursors for preparing CQDs by the nitric acid reflux method. Scheme 1 showed the CQDs prepared using toluene as the solvent exhibited the highest fluorescence quantum efficiency of 7.42%. A novel “on-off” fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) nanosensor was obtained to quantitatively characterize Cu2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs decreases due to reacting with Fe3+ and the fluorescence of the N-CQDs + Fe3+ composite system can be regained using L-ascorbic acid. Therefore, the CQDs-Fe3+ system can serve as an “off-on” fluorescence sensor to determine the concentration of AA in several drugs. The prepared N-CQDs exhibited high sensitivity, good selectivity, and low cost. Overall, coal tar pitch components were first obtained as the carbon source by using an economical, simple, and green method to prepare water-soluble N-CQDs. Therefore, this work demonstrates one of the various possibilities in the high value-added utilization of coal tar pitch.

All the metal salts Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (99.0%), FeCl3·6H2O (98.0%), CaCl2 (99.9%), PbCl2 (99.0%), SrCl2·6H2O (99.0%), MgCl2·6H2O (98.0%), AlCl3 (99.0%), HgCl2 (99.5%), NaCl (99.8%), KCl (99.8%), LiCl (97.0%), BaCl2 (99.5%), SnCl2·2H2O (98.0%), NiSO4·6H2O (98.5%), Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (98.0%), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (98.5%), AgNO3 (99.8%), FeSO4·7H2O (99.0%), nitric acid (65.0 ~ 68.0%), sodium hydroxide (96.0%) and L-ascorbic acid (99%) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Baihekang Brand Vitamin C Lozenge was obtained from Weihai BAIHE biotechnology co. LTD. All chemical reagents were analytical grade.

Medium-low temperature coal tar pitch (ML-CTP) was acquired from Inner Mongolia Qinghua group co. Ltd. N-heptane, toluene, and quinoline were purchased from Beijing Chemical Industry Group Co. Ltd (Beijing, China) and Shenyang Dongxing Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shenyang, China).

The separation of medium-low temperature coal tar pitch components was performed using a solvent extraction method with n-heptane, toluene, and quinoline as extracting agents. The four components obtained by stepped extraction were: a heptane-soluble component (HS), a toluene-soluble component (TS), a quinolone-soluble component (QS), and a quinolone-insoluble component (QI). The QI component was almost non-existent.

N-CQDs were synthesized using a chemical oxidation method, as reported in the literature. First, 1.00 ± 0.02 g of the components extracted from the medium-low temperature coal tar pitch and 150 mL of a 5 mol/L nitric acid solution were decanted into a 250 mL three-necked round flask. Subsequently, the flask was placed in a magnetic heating stirrer with a silicone oil bath and heated at 120 °C for 11 h while stirring. After processing, the resulted brown solution was poured into a beaker and cooled to room temperature. The colored solution was successively centrifuged at 4000 rpm and 10000 rpm to remove unreacted ingredients and the supernatant from the second centrifugation was neutralized. The neutralized solution was put into dialysis bag (MW cut off 3500 Da) for dialysis against deionized water for 72 h to obtain a high purity N-CQD solution. The pure aqueous solution was evaporated and concentrated to a small volume, and finally dried at 30 °C.

The morphology of the nitrogen-doped CQDs was analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2100, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Information about the Valence state of the elements was obtained by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK) with mono Al-Kα as the excitation source (1486.6 eV). The characterization of the molecular structure was done using an FT-IR (Thermo Scientific Nicolet) spectrometer manufactured by Thermo Scientific, USA. The Raman spectrum was used to examine the graphitization of CQDs (XploRA PLUS from Horiba Scientific (French)). A Perkin Elmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (USA) was used to obtain the fluorescence spectra, while a Perkin Elmer Lambda 900 UV/VIS/NIR spectrophotometer (USA) was used for the UV–vis absorption spectra.

The quantum yield (QY) was measured by comparing the absorbency values and integrated photoluminescence intensities of the N-CQDs and the reference sample (quinine sulfate). The solutions of quinine sulfate and CQDs were excited by 360 nm UV-light, and their absorption and emission spectra were measured and analyzed. The QY of quinine sulfate dissolved in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution was 0.54 at 360 nm UV-light. The N-CQDs were dissolved in deionized water. The quantum yield of the N-CQDs was calculated according to Eq. (1).
(1)
Y u
=
Y s
·
F u
/
F s
·
(
A s
/
A u
)
where Ys and Yu refer to the quantum yield of quinine sulfate and N-CQDs, respectively, Fs and Fu are the integrated photoluminescence intensities of quinine sulfate and N-CQDs, respectively, As and Au refer to the absorbance at 360 nm of quinine sulfate and N-CQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs by Cu2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: 3 mL of N-CQD aqueous dispersion were poured into a cuvette. In quick succession, 3 μL of Cu2+, Fe3+ solutions and 0.1 mol/L of additional ions (Cr3+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Li+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Al3+, and Fe2+) were incorporated. The emission spectra of the mixtures were measured at room temperature (λex = 365 nm) after being stirred for at least 30 s.

3 mL of the aqueous solution containing 80 μM Fe3+ and N-CQDs (0.03 g/L) were injected to a cuvette. In rapid sequence, different concentrations of L-ascorbic acid solution were injected to the cuvette containing the N-CQDs/Fe3+ solution. After being stirred for half a minute, the emission spectra were measured at room temperature. All of the above tests were performed using an excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

Water samples were gathered from the Wanshui River and the Yingze Lake in Liaoning, China. Additionally, a tap water sample was obtained from the laboratory. Before detection, the three samples were filtered to remove insoluble particles and were left undisturbed at room temperature for 24 h.

The practicability of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system to detect L-ascorbic acid was evaluated by the analysis of vitamin C tablets. The medicine was dissolved in a 100 mL volumetric flask with boiled deionized water, without any further processing, and quickly measured. Finally, the emission spectra were collected at λex = 365 nm.

In the present work, N-CQDs were prepared by a nitric acid reflux method using extracted components from the medium-low temperature coal tar pitch. The synthesis conditions such as the concentration of nitric acid, reaction time, and temperature were optimized. As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescence spectra of the three kinds of carbon quantum dots at a wavelength of 365 nm showed that the intensity gradually increased when the mass concentration of carbon quantum dots increased from 0.01 g/L to 0.04 g/L, and the intensity of the CQDs derived from TS was the highest at equal concentrations. The highest QY of 7.42% was obtained from carbon quantum dots synthesized using 1.003 g of TS and 5 mol/L of HNO3 solution as raw materials, 120 °C as the reaction temperature, and 11 h as the reaction time. This material was named N-CQDs and was used for the remaining discussion in this report. The QY of CQDs obtained from HS and QS were 6.88% and 4.14%, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is the best detection method for the morphology of carbon quantum dots. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the TEM image reveals a relatively uniform and well-dispersed spherical morphology with a statistical average diameter of 0.79 ± 0.2 nm, as observed in Fig. 2B, which is smaller than previously reported in the literature. The FT-IR spectrum verified the presence of many different functional groups in the surface of the obtained N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, the peak at 3327 cm−1 with a strong and broad absorption band, arises from the stretching vibrations of OH. The shape and position of this broad peak indicate the presence of hydroxyl groups branching from the surface of the N-CQDs. Furthermore, this broad peak may also involve to the stretching vibration of NH. The small peak at 2984 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CH, and the two peaks located at 1561 cm−1 and 1359 cm−1 correspond to the NO2 group, which possess anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations respectively. The peak at 1263 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN or COC bonds. The band at 1062 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO bonds. These oxygenic and nitrogenous functional groups lead to the favorable hydrophilicity of the obtained N-CQDs. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 2D) displays the two bands of D and G at 1361 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1, respectively. The D-band reflects the defect or edge of the carbon atom lattice, while the G-band signifies the stretching vibration mode of the sp2 bonded carbon-carbon in the plane. The ratio of the relative intensity of the two bands (ID/IG) was 1.08, which suggests that N-CQDs contain sp2 carbon clusters and possess a partially defect structure of the carbon atom lattice [18,36].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to characterize the elements and the chemical bonds of the N-CQDs. The full XPS spectrum laid out three dominant peaks at 284.6 eV, 399.7 eV, and 531.4 eV, as seen in Fig. 3 A, which reveal that the N-CQDs were primarily comprised of C, O, and N. Moreover, the composition analysis of the three elements in atomic percentages in the N-CQDs was 68.21%, 27.37%, and 4.42%, respectively. More specifically, the high resolution of the C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) can be divided into four peaks located at 284.3, 284.9, 285.4, and 288.2 eV, which originated from CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO/CN groups, respectively [37]. In addition, the high-resolution of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) was fitted to four peaks at 399.2, 399.9, 400.5, and 406 eV, which consisted of CNC, CNC, amino-N, and nitro-N groups, respectively [38]. Last, from the O1s spectrum in Fig. 3D, the three peaks located at 530.9, 531.6, and 532.6 eV can be attributed to CO, COH, and CO groups, respectively [39]. The results from FT-IR and XPS confirm the N-CQDs exhibited COOH, OH, and NO2 functional groups [40].

The optical characteristics of the N-CQDs were analyzed by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectophotometry. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 A, N-CQDs exhibited strong absorbance in the UV region, further extending into the visible region [41]. The absorption spectrum displays two characteristic peaks at 221 nm and 260 nm, which are consistent with previous literature reports [42]. The former may be generated from the π − π* transition of the continuous carbon-carbon conjugated structure double bonds [43], while the latter one can be attributed to n-π* transition originating from CO/C N [40,44,45]. As demonstrated in Fig. 4A, the apexes of the excitation and emission spectra peaks can be observed at 337 nm and 449 nm. Moreover, it can be determined from the inset in Fig. 3A that the solution of the dissolved N-CQDs is light brown, transparent, and clear in broad daylight and emits brilliant blue fluorescence under the 365 nm excitation wavelength [32]. CQDs possess fascinating fluorescence properties, but the origin of the luminescence is still unclear. Fig. 4B shows the emission spectra acquired with different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 470 nm with a 10 nm increment. It can be observed that the apex of the emission peaks exhibit a noticeable red-shift from 417 to 530 nm and the intensity decreases, as shown in the red dotted line in the figure [46,47]. This property is excitation-dependent and may result from the oxygenous and nitrogenous groups present in N-CQDs.

Fig. 4C shows the fluorescence spectra of the N-CQDs solutions with different concentrations at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The maximum emission wavelength of these four concentrations is approximately 449 nm. Thus, the effect of concentration in the range of 0.01–0.04 g/L on the position of maximum fluorescence emission wavelength can be ignored. The influence of hydrogen potential on the FL intensity of N-CQDs at an excitation of 365 nm is shown in Fig. 4D. It can be observed that the FL intensity increased gradually within the pH range of 1.0–3.0. The fluorescence intensity remained nearly constant in the pH range of 4–10 and, on the contrary, gradually decreased in the pH range of 10–14. For the N-CQDs with acidic or basic groups like OH, COOH, and NH2, the dissociation of acidic groups and the protonation of basic groups may change the nature and rate of the non-radiative transition process competing with the luminescence process, which affects the emission spectra, including its intensity and shape [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of N-CQDs to Cu2+and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Ion detection is one of the most common applications of carbon quantum dots [12,48]. Considering that the fluorescence intensity remained nearly constant in the pH values ranging from 4 to 10, the ion detection environment is determined to be neutral without the buffer solution. Moreover, the disappearance of fluorescence which is presented in Fig. 5 B confirmed that the existence of Cu2+ and Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs. The selectivity of the obtained N-CQDs towards Cu2+ and Fe3+ was analyzed by observing the FL intensity in the presence of a variety of ions. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, Cu2+ and Fe3+ decreased the FL intensity while no particularly noticeable changes in the F/F0 of the N-CQDs were observed after immitting 100 μM of other common metal ions (Ag+, Li+, Al3+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, K+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Sn2+). These achieved results suggest that the N-CQDs are suitable for monitoring Cu2+ and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CQDs for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the applicability of the developed N-CQDs in practical fields, the interference of other common ions on the quenching of Cu2+ and Fe3+ was investigated. As presented in Fig. 6 A and B, when other metal ions coexist with Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively, the interference effect can be neglected [49]. These results indicate that the N-CQDs hold a good identifiability for Cu2+ and Fe3+ detection without the interference of other metal ions.

N-CQDs can act as fluorescent sensors to detect the content of Fe3+ and Cu2+. The fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CQDs solution along with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ are portrayed in Fig. 7 A and C. And, after reacting for 30 s at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were measured under excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The intensity gradually decreased following increase of Fe3+ and Cu2+ concentration with invariability of the spectra's shape and position.

The relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity values and the concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ is given by the Stern-Volmer equation as follow:
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K S
[ Q ]

In the Eq. (2), F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of the ions, respectively, [Q] refers to the concentration of Fe3+ and Cu2+, and Ks represents the quenching association constant.

The fitting curves coming from the ratios of F0/F and the concentration of the two cations exhibited good linearity over the range of 1–50 μM with correlation coefficients of 0.9955 and 0.9944 for Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. The limits of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were calculated to be 0.173 μM and 0.16 μM, according to the equation 3σ/K, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 11) and K represents the calculated slope of the linear fitted curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In Section 3.4, including Figs. 5–7, we have concluded that Fe3+ and Cu2+ can quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs aqueous solution without mutual interference with other ions, and there are linear relationship between the concentration of ions (Fe3+ and Cu2+) and F0/F, which means that N-CQDs can be used to selectively detect Fe3+ and Cu2+.The practicality of the N-CQDs to detect Cu2+and Fe3+ in real water samples from Wanshui River, Yingze Lake, and tap water was evaluated. The real aqueous samples were orderly filtered through a 0.22 membrane, after that, left undisturbed for 24 h. Different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ (5, 10, and 20 μM) were added to the three processed samples, then the fluorescence spectra were measured at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Moreover, the experiment was performed three times under the same conditions to obtain the relative errors(REs) the relative standard deviations(RSDs). The smaller the two values, the higher the accuracy and precision of the results, which means that the results are closer to the true values. As shown in Table 1 , the REs (n = 3) of Fe3+ in the real samples were within the scope of 0.15%~7.80%, and the RSDs (n = 3) were smaller than 4.09%. Additionally, Table 2 shows that the boundary values of the REs (n = 3) of Cu2+ in real samples was 0.35% and 4.40%, and the RSDs (n = 3) were less than 6.52%. From the data in the Tables 1 and 2, almost all REs and RSDs of the two ions are less than 5%, which suggests that the accuracy and precision is very satisfactory. And the accuracr is higher at high concentrations (10, 20 μM) compared with low concentrations (5 μM). Therefore, N-CQDs exhibited an acceptable performance in real samples as confirmed by fluorescence analysis and it can be used to effectively detect the content of Fe3+and Cu2+ in natural water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CQDs-Fe3+ for the detection of AA
ce:section.ce:para:
Fe3+ is a highly oxidizing particle, which easily reacts with reducing substances, like AA. Thus, a redox reaction occurs between Fe3+ and AA leading to the oxidation of the enediol group in AA together with the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ (Eq. (3)). For this reason, a Fe3+ and N-CQDs composite sensor can be designed to detect AA in drugs.
(3)

To investigate and verify the amount of L-ascorbic acid in drugs using this composite material, many groups of experiments were performed and the excitation wavelength of 365 nm was used in all the experiments. As arranged in Fig. 8 A, the effect of L-ascorbic acid and vitamin C drugs on the fluorescence of the as-prepared N-CQDs solution was examined, and it was determined that the microvariations on intensity were irrespective. The fluorescence intensity of quenched N-CQDs solution was gradually recovered with the increase of ascorbic acid as L-ascorbic acid reacted with Fe3+ to form Fe2+ based on Eq. (3), instead of reacting with Cu2+, as displayed in Fig. 8B. Therefore, based on the off-onon phenomenon of the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs + Fe3+ system, an off-on type fluorescence sensor for selectively measuring AA can be constructed. As shown in Fig. 8C, the quenched fluorescence gradually recovered with increasing isoconcentration of L-ascorbic acid. Based on Fig. 8C, the relationship between the recovered fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of L-ascorbic acid was determined, which shows a good fitted linear correlation with an R2 value being 0.9918 in Fig. 8D. The linear equation is F = 7.6877x + 231.9658, and x is the concentration of L-ascorbic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Practical applications of the hybrid system for L-ascorbic acid assays were verified by the direct analysis of vitamin C drugs, and the quantity of AA in such tablets was determined according to the Section 3.6. The excitation wavelength is also 365 nm in the all experiment. Table 3 compares the experimental results against known amounts in the samples, which shows that the REs of AA ranged from 0.48% to 1.35%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were lower than 1.5%. Moreover, the satisfactory results obtained were almost equivalent to the reference values on the drug labels, as shown in Table 3 and the RE ≤ 1.35% and RSD < 1.5%(n = 3) confirm the practical application of the sensor system for AA quantification. The experiment was performed three times under the same experimental conditions to obtain the relative errors(REs) and the relative standard deviations(RSDs).

FT-IR and XPS results demonstrated that N-CQDs exhibit oxygenic functional groups with negative charge [50], such as CO, COOH, OH and NO2, NH2 groups [51,52], as shown in Fig. 9 A. Moreover, these functional groups have a structure comparable to that of EDTA, which provides a large number of lone pairs of electrons and the possibility of coordination [53]. In this work, the FL intensity of the prepared N-CQDs solution gradually decreased with increasing concentration of Fe3+ and Cu2+. Fig. 9B shows the absorption spectra of the N-CQDs, N-CQDs + Fe3+, and N-CQDs + Cu2+ solutions. Additionally, the formation of ground state complexes affects the absorption spectra of fluorescent substances, and the way to discern between static and dynamic quenching is by elaborately analyzing the absorption spectra of the carbon quantum dots and the mixed solution [54]. It can be observed from Fig. 9B that before 300 nm, the curve corresponding to N-CQDs exhibits two distinct absorption peaks, while the others do not. Moreover, the curve corresponding to N-CQDs is smooth at approximately 350 nm, and the other two have a slight bulge. This difference in the absorption spectrum is due to static quenching. Therefore, it is speculated that the reason behind fluorescence quenching is the formation of non-luminous ground state complexes of N-CQDs with Cu2+ and Fe3+, in which the electrons of the complexes return to the ground state without any photons being emitted under UV irradiation. The reason behind fluorescence recovery is that L-ascorbic acid takes away Fe3+ from the non-luminescent complex, and the two undergo a redox reaction to produce more stable and lower energy substances. The N-CQDs return to the original state, so fluorescence reappears.

In summary, carbon quantum dots with outstanding fluorescence have been prepared by a chemical oxidation method from heptane insoluble but toluene soluble (HI-TS) components derived from the medium-low temperature coal tar pitch as the carbon source. This nitrogen-doped CQDs were successfully utilized as a highly sensitive and selective sensor towards Cu2+ and Fe3+ detection based on a fluorescence on-off switch model. Moreover, the Fe3+ and N-CQDs composite sensing system was successfully applied for the detection of L-ascorbic acid based on the fluorescence off-on switch model. Therefore, the developed N-CQDs have immense potential not only for their application as Cu2+ and Fe3+ sensors but also for detecting L-ascorbic acid in drugs as an eco-friendly sensor. Additionally, this study enlightens new possible research directions for the high value-added utilization of coal tar pitch. The formation of ground state complexes and the occurrence of REDOX reactions are the basic principle for the detection of Cu2+, Fe3+, and L-ascorbic acid by N-CQDs. However, the quantum yield of this N-CQDs and the relationship between the products and the coal tar pitch need to be further improved and explored.

Chaoshuai Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yaming Zhu: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xuefei Zhao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1361126 and 51874169), the Nature Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (20180551218), the Excellent Talents Training Program of the Education Department of Liaoning Province (2020LNQN03), the Excellent Talents Training Program of University of Science and Technology Liaoning (2018RC07), Graduate Education Reform and Science Technology Innovation Entrepreneurship Project of University of Science and Technology Liaoning (LKDYC201802).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, X. Yang, N. Wang, J. Lv, H. Wang, M.M.F. Choi, W. Bian, Graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots as an “off-on” fluorescent switch for determination of mercury(II) and sulfide, Mikrochim. Acta 185 (2018) 471. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604-018-2994-0.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Guo, F. Cao, Y. Li, Solid phase synthesis of nitrogen and phosphor co-doped carbon quantum dots for sensing Fe3+ and the enhanced photocatalytic degradation of dyes, Sens. Actuators, B 255 (2018) 1105-1111. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.104.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Pourreza, M. Ghomi, Green synthesized carbon quantum dots from Prosopis juliflora leaves as a dual off-on fluorescence probe for sensing mercury (II) and chemet drug, Mat. Sci. Eng. C-Mater. 98 (2019) 887-896. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.12.141.

Sci. 469 (2019) 911-916. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.11.095.
